speaker: interviewer
text: June 1st, 1995. This is tape 5. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to the railroad station, with all-- with that-- with the new name. Even I-- I met that guy from the same city. Mark, Mark, Mark! I said, listen, I have to go someplace, I cannot talk to you. I haven't got the time. I have the-- But anyway, I make it-- make it a little bit short, because it's a long story, actually. And the-- the car, Warsaw-Stockholm, was the last car which used to go-- it was vacation time, and about 10 cars, or 12 cars that used to go to the-- to the Baltic Sea. And I-- and the people were rushing into the car, to get the-- because they used to give out more tickets than people. And I was waiting someplace, when everybody went into the-- I went first into the car, Warsaw-Stockholm. The guy saluted to me, you know. And even-- I was afraid-- even going out to railroad, I bought a Polish ticket, because I was afraid, even somebody would see that I'm showing a foreign passport. But anyway, I make it very short. Then I came-- I went into the-- this was a few-- about 15 minutes. I covered the window-- because it was a sleeping car, I covered the windows with the curtain, not that anybody be able to-- and the Scandinavian people were talking, but they were the-- but they were not like me. I was afraid. And, took a long-- finally, the car moved. And I told the guy that I'm a nervous guy. Then he said to me-- all of a sudden, he had some Valium or something. I didn't know, at that time, what Valium is or anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, take one pill before you go to sleep at night. And in the morning, when you get up, take the other one. Yes? And I slept very well. You know? I was young. And even so, I was excited-- I slept.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to the-- to the-- to the ocean-- to the no, to the-- to the-- to the sea-- Baltic Sea. Oh, I'm telling you-- so much noise. Because this was the border, and so much-- so many dogs, you know, barking. And then two-- two Polish officers came in-- give-- show-- give me your passport. I gave him my passport. I looked-- I looked at him with one eye, and one eye, I was reading the-- the Austrian-- the Austrian magazine. And I make myself believe that I don't care too much about what he's talking to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took away from me the passport. Then, they came in, those guys, who take care on-- money, or the luggage. He said, open the luggage. And then I said, OK. Because they were very nice to the other countries-- showed them that they are very democratic, you know? Toward-- to their own people, they're very rough. No, he said to me, do you have any money? And this guy gave-- the diplomat, gave me some money. I showed that. And he said, ah, that's nothing. It's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then came three soldiers. And they said, get out of your seat. They took off the seat-- they were thinking maybe somebody is under the seat. And they took off-- Of course, take it sometimes. Then the captain who took my passport, he came back. I recognized by the collar. I was-- I felt very strong after the-- after the Valium or whatever. I pulled out my own passport. And it took some times, you know, and went into East Germany. I still was afraid, but East Germany was still communist. Then we crossed East Germany-- went through the West Germany. Even a West German soldier said to me, have a-- nice trip. And then we came to the-- to the-- you know, like you go to Staten Island-- how do you call it--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ferry?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ferry. And they took that-- that last car, put into the ship, which have railroads, you know? And I was so thirsty, because he gave me salami, bread, chocolate. And I was afraid to go into the restaurant, because-- you know. And I left everything, and I went on the ship. I was very happy. Yes, and also the guy told me that, at the-- at the sea, somebody-- two people who wait for me and will say, [NON-ENGLISH], which means those guys who will take care of me, because I am in Sweden already. And they gave me some chocolate, flowers. They said, listen, you're going to Israel. It won't be such easy life, but we got you out of Poland, from the system. Of course. And they said, this will take a whole night until you'll get to Stockholm. It's a long way from there. And in Stockholm, some other people will wait for you, and say some signals. And then, if they say that signal, go to them, and be with them. And, of course, again, the whole night I slept. I came. I got those people. They took me immediately to a hotel. I didn't have any identifications. He said-- one day they told me, you know, we will go to a store, to a five-, ten-cent store, take a picture, and give you some ID card. In the meantime, they gave me money, I had good food in a good restaurant in Stockholm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they said, we will send you by plane to Israel. I said, what's the rush? I never flew a plane. I was afraid. I said, I can go by boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, no, no, no, no, no. You have to go by plane. And-- you know, and then they took me to the airport. And the guy-- one of the diplomats, he said, we will see, and you be sure where I am. If you will have any difficulties, because you don't know the language in Sweden, just-- with your finger, and we will straighten out. You know Sweden-- it's a free country. I didn't have any problems. They took the ticket, and even didn't ask for an ID card. And I flew-- I flew to Israel. With, of course, stops in Copenhagen, in Dusseldorf, Vienna, you know, some other island. No, I-- and back to Tel Aviv. And, of-- the plane came in the middle of the night. And I got very, very, you know, nervous, because I saw those lights, and I am-- here I was in Poland, and now I'm in-- free in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they kept me overnight, because even in Israel they didn't want to let the people know that people were running away from Poland, because everything was done illegally. You understand? Then they kept me in the airport overnight. And in the morning, special people-- police or FBI people-- came in. They took me out from the airport. The back-- the back-- not the front from the airport, the back, you know? Because normally I wouldn't be able to go out there. And they took me to a special place-- a chicken farm. And also this guy told me, if you meet people-- how you get to Israel? Just say that you tried different ways. They gave you a shot, and you don't know. You woke up, and you were in Czechoslovakia. You know? You know, it's unbelievable stories, you know? I wish I could spend the real time, then it would take hours-- hours.

speaker: interviewee
text: Already, I am in the chicken farm. I'm getting everything. He comes every day to me-- brings me Polish papers. Because in Israel, you have printed Polish newspapers. He brought me, you know, even shoes--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this person helping you, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything was the organizations. I wouldn't like to say too much about it, because, you know, it's a little bit this-- you know, it shouldn't be said too much about it. And, little by little, you know, I find out-- I went into Tel Aviv. I found friends. Friends took me there. I worked a little bit as a dishwasher. You know-- different way. My brother used to support me-- send me some money-- to support me. The friend didn't want even to take the money from me. And everything was for free. And my brother was trying very hard to get a visa to the United States-- a preference visa, because he married the American lady, and he automatically-- now, it's not that way. He automatically became an American citizen. And I-- it took me a little more than a year being in Israel, and I came to the United States. Being in the-- on the boat, coming from Israel by boat to the United--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was thinking, what I'm going to do? I haven't-- I am not like my brother, a watchmaker. I am an accountant. I don't know English. What I'm going to do? At that time, I started to realize, what I'm going to do in this-- in the United States? I have no trade, no profession. And, you know, I have-- I'm-- again, very short-- I was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Four years, I was suffering, going from one factory to another. I used to be a messenger. I was not good, because people couldn't understand me, and I couldn't understand people. And finally, through friends, I got into a place where I worked for 31 years, at a real-estate office. They treated me very nicely. I used to make good money. I used to save-- My wife which I married in this country-- the first wife-- we both worked, and one salary was-- we kept for the rent and you know. And then unfortunately, being with her 18 years, she became very ill. She got the multiple myeloma-- very terrible disease.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was-- you know, I am so-- Lillian Frenkel. That was her-- her maiden name. And she was also not before-- never married. But she was in a German concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell the name for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lillian-- I don't believe I don't have to spell it, Lillian. Frenkel-- F-R-E-N-K-E-L. And she was from Lodz, the second-largest city in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you came to the United States, what-- where was your brother living? What state?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother had already a store, because his wife was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Washington Heights. 200 Street. I forgot again. The 200th Street and Broadway.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York.

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York. And, he-- you know, he was a good-- a good trade. People used to like him. He was an honest man. Ladies would come to fix watches, to make up new rings. He used to make a good living. And he had one son. And he became-- you know, he educated him. He became a doctor. He's alive, he's married, he's in Philadelphia, have three kids-- three sons.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what else I can tell you? I was very lucky that I got this job, what I used to be-- 31 years. They liked me very much. And thanks to them, I am in this apartment, because otherwise I would live in Brooklyn, or in Bronx, or in Queens. What company was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they treated me very nice, because they felt that I am a good worker, and you know, very trustworthy, and also, how would you say, very devoted-- devoted worker. You know? And I was very happy there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What company was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Carol Management Company. They used to be on 600 Madison Avenue. Very nice people. They treated me unusually nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Carol Management.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yes. I used to go to Florida. They used to give me the best-- the best hotel-- the Royal Beach Hotel-- for very little money-- for $30. The hotel and the food and everything. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it you came here, to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it that you came to the US?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did my wife--

speaker: interviewer
text: --did you come to the US-- what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, year. I came in 1954-- January 31, 1956-- I'm sorry. I left Poland in 1954. And to the United States, I came in January 31, 1956. And after five years, I became an American-- a US citizen. And, of course, as I told you, my wife-- in 1980 she passed away. [CRYING] [DRINKS] One second. I remarried about two years later, to Barbara Weber. Her husband was a dentist. And she came to this country with him. He was already had a health problem. And after two years being in this country, he passed away. And she was left with a son. She wasn't very-- and she was on three jobs, because she wanted to give to the son education. And at that time, it was the Vietnam War. She was very much afraid that they will take away from her. And eventually, it happened that he was not mobilized. And I am with her from 1980. And she works, even now, one day a week, as a dental assistant. And they like her, because she knows the trade. And just ask me a few questions, because I am excited. [LAUGHS] And nervous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you a few questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I-- again, I will tell the whole story. If I could tell this in Yiddish and Polish, it would be a different ball game.

speaker: interviewer
text: You actually could--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK-- well, OK--

speaker: interviewer
text: --could do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever I could, I did. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many family members did you lose in the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I would say, even from my father's side, there were four brothers and four sisters-- in different towns in Poland-- in Lodz, in Lomza, in Warsaw, in Biezun. And each one has maybe four or five or six kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: The mother's side were five, you know? And also-- and everybody, except me and my brother-- which I called him in Warsaw to run away-- everybody got killed. Everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Some day I figured out it was over 70 people, counting uncles, aunts, and cousins. Of course, those uncles and aunts, they wouldn't be alive today. But some cousins. And that's what my problem is now-- for the last 10 years, when I go to sleep, I see all those cousins of the-- the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And thinking back on the war years--

speaker: interviewee
text: On what?

speaker: interviewer
text: On the war years.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you figure out what sustained you, mentally?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What sustained you? What kept you going, mentally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mentally? I believe-- I-- normally, I am not that strong, mentally. But I did a good job. I am-- I am surprised of myself. Now, I am not doing so well. Now, I am a very poor sleeper. If I don't take a sleeping pill, I don't sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: But physically, I'm still all right. I am still strong at my age. You know, God is good to me. That's what I can say. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was times that I was in terrible-- very terrible situations. But, as I said, God was good to me. I'm alive. But I feel sorry that I am the only one [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say there was anything that you did in the war that was against your nature, or something that you'd be ashamed of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I didn't like to be in Poland, because some of those friends, they were-- I don't know whether to make career, or to be-- I was not on their side. They treated me as an enemy. They couldn't do-- they couldn't put me into the jail. But even-- even when I left Poland illegally, I used to get letters that the-- in the town where I used to live. They treated-- they used to say that I am a hero. If I could-- that Rosenthal, who was not such mentally so strong, and could do that job at that time, when most of those big shots in Poland were Russians, like the-- secretary of the-- the secretary of the war-- the war secretary-- Rokossovsky. That at that time, that I could leave Poland illegally, that I am a hero.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the time, people were talking constantly-- of course, very-- [NON-ENGLISH] used to have trust, or they used to talk about me that-- how could I-- how could I leave Poland at that time, when the border was completely with all electronics-- how did he get out? It's unbelievable. Is there anything you did that you were ashamed of?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I would have to do it again?

speaker: interviewer
text: That you are ashamed of. Did you ever--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I am not ashamed. The other way. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: About anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to say to them, listen, I said a little bit, which is-- I said, I didn't-- my parents were very poor. I was not a capitalist. I want to leave Poland not because I don't like that system, because actually I didn't like that system, but I said to them, I don't like it, and not because I don't like the system. I want to join my brother. My whole family perished. I want to be with my brother. That's my aim, and nobody can take away this from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would do differently, that you did during any of those years? Is there anything you would do differently?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean in what respect?

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything. Did you ever have to pay somebody off and you regret that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was-- no, I am very happy that the United States let me in. I am very grateful. Because, after all-- you know? I am very, very grateful that they let me in. And, after all, I didn't have no business. Everything what I possess, is just what I earned. And everything was paid out-- deducted. Every dollar what I saved was paid to the US government.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm-- I am happy-- inside. You're proud.

speaker: interviewee
text: Inside, I'm happy. Maybe I don't look so happy, [LAUGH] outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, you look very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah? But-- but I didn't do it the same like other fellows, they're in real estate-- they make millions. I am comfortable. I am not rich. I cannot afford those things what they do. But I am happy. My wife is a lovely person. She likes to go places, and we go places. And we're enjoying and wherever-- any show we go to do, even a walking distance. We go to the museum, we go to movies, we go to hotels. We travel-- we went to Europe. We went to-- even to Africa, to Morocco. We went to the place where Rambam Maimonides used to live. I used to see the house where he used to live. And I have been in Israel three times, already. And I hope, if the health-- if I will be healthy, probably I will go again, to visit. Even so-- if I don't have any family in Israel, but I'll try to go and visit again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're proud.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh? [LAUGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: You're proud.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That somebody got to say. [LAUGH]

speaker: interviewer
text: You're allowed to say it.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're allowed to say that you're proud of your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have two more questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your experiences affect your religious beliefs?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, what happened-- I was with my father, it was a tradition. I was not convinced, completely, to be-- but I follow. I wanted to be a good son. And if it was a holiday, I used to go with him to the shul, to the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: But after the war-- I-- I don't make this as a big plus. But-- circumstances, you know? And I feel sorry. I would feel much better if I would, you know, continue the way I used to be as a young man. But the circumstances.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish I would be really a religious man. But I am not. But I am a Jew. I feel-- national-- nationalistically, I am a Jew. And I wish-- I would like, during my lifetime, to see Israel as a-- have peace. Be peace with those neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this experience-- you know, being through the war-- change your view on human beings, in general?

speaker: interviewee
text: On what? I'm losing my hearing a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your experiences with the war change your view of human beings, in general?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. First of all, I'm very anti-Soviet, anti-communist. Because, you know, it's written different, and you know, the way they treated innocent people-- it's unbelievable. Unbelievable. The way even--

speaker: interviewee
text: When it was their freedom, you couldn't-- I remember a little thing. I used to be in the office. They didn't have paper. And they used to have those little, you know, little, little leaflets about the politics, you know? And on one side was blank, and on the other side was Stalin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I needed paper. I didn't even saw-- and I put-- made it in two. And somebody let this known to the politburo. Not 'politburo'-- the politician. Not politician-- that communist was on top. And he called me to his office. This guy was an exception-- a nice guy. He said, Mark, never in the future, when you see the picture of Stalin-- and don't make it in two pieces on the other, because you will get into trouble. [LAUGH] But anyway, that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's not the system-- it didn't prove well, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about people in general?

speaker: interviewee
text: People are nice. I would say, when I was in Middle Asia, and in very bad conditions, and I used to work as a-- you know in a restaurant, and to make fire outside the building. And I remember a Russian-- a Russian woman came to me and brought me food. She knew that I was hungry, and she brought me food-- gave me as much as she could. But she, you know, divided-- what-- she shared with me. But she would bring me-- And I would say the Russian people are nice people. I would prefer them better than-- I shouldn't say that-- my native people, in my country, Poland. Very nice people. They treated me exceptionally nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and finally, are there any-- any words of wisdom you would like to leave us with? Any philosophy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Philosophy? I am excited. I cannot concentrate myself on this. Normally I would. But I'm-- I'm-- because the age, you know? If I would be at the age when I was in the Soviet-- in the concentration-- I would give you, immediately. Now, it's very tough for me, because I'm really, really excited. It doesn't maybe show up on my face, but I am nervous, I'm excited, and-- and sometimes I don't say it the way I would like to say.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, one more question-- I promise-- last question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- please, please.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the most difficult decision you had to make?

speaker: interviewee
text: The most difficult decision was to run away from Poland illegally. Because I would get in big, big trouble. Something like this happen in-- in-- in Romania. And let them-- and the government knew that this guy has a passport like I had. Even I have that paper. I still have the paper-- Israel was it printed. And they let him go to the border. He went into the train in Bucharest. But they let-- and on the-- on the border, they caught him. And, of course, I don't know what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know that the diplomat became persona non grata, and got to leave Romania. Because-- because he said the way he-- you know, they have ways to get out of you, how did you get that passport? How did you get that visa? And everything. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That way, I was lucky that I didn't-- if I would fall in the position like this guy, I don't know whether I would be today here and giving your interview.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to add, before we end?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my mother and father would be-- [CRYING] --could see me, you know, at least for an hour. In those conditions-- in this country. [CRYING] [DRINKS] I feel [SOBBING] that my parents had a very bad time. Before they got killed, they were thinking, where is Mark, and-- [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] I am sure that, when they went to the gas chambers, because they didn't know anything about me. And you know, you're a mother-- you know what it means for a mother about her kids. At that time, I was maybe 20, 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure somehow they know.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sure somehow they know.

speaker: interviewee
text: Eh-- I-- they were very good parents, even in that-- poor conditions. But very-- a lot of love. A lot of everything what they could. They used to do more than they could, in those circumstances, especially Mother.
